coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanostructures in polyvinyl alcohol-based silver nanocomposites for serum albumin concentration sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: V.C., Priyamvada
Udayan, Sony
K.R., Vijesh
Nampoori, V.P.N.
P., Radhakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: Introducing carbon nanostructures in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix as strengthening elements by solution dispersion method is already known. Here, the plasmonic sensor surfaces interspersed with carbon nanomaterials are studied for their durable and better sensing capabilities. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) and Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) are the carbon nanostructures used in the polyvinyl alcohol-silver nanocomposite to develop plasmonic sensing surfaces. Fiber optic unclad U-bent surfaces are encapsulated in the carbon nanostructure dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite. The surface is further functionalized with the polyelectrolyte poly(allylamine) hydrochloride (PAH) for better bioconjugation. In the available sensor literature, the evaluation of the sensing layer stability after multiple sensing trials is almost nil. In this work, the sensor surface plasmonic performance after multiple sensing trials is evaluated. The results show that plasmonic biosensors with improved sensing performances can be developed by introducing carbon nanomaterials in PVA-silver nanocomposites. The developed sensor heads are used to sense Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) concentration changes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVA-silver nanocomposite
Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD)
Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT)

Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) in serum albumin sensing
MWCNT dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite sensor-head fabrication

row.entry: U bent fiber optic LSPR biosensor

head.ce:title: Carbon nanostructures in polyvinyl alcohol-based silver nanocomposites for serum albumin concentration sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International School of Photonics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, Kerala 682 022, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International School of Photonics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, Kerala 682 022, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CRANN, Trinity College, The University of Dublin, Ireland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CRANN, Trinity College, The University of Dublin, Ireland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological Sciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological Sciences and Bioengineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biological Sciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Introducing carbon nanostructures in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix as strengthening elements by solution dispersion method is already known. Here, the plasmonic sensor surfaces interspersed with carbon nanomaterials are studied for their durable and better sensing capabilities. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) and Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) are the carbon nanostructures used in the polyvinyl alcohol-silver nanocomposite to develop plasmonic sensing surfaces. Fiber optic unclad U-bent surfaces are encapsulated in the carbon nanostructure dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite. The surface is further functionalized with the polyelectrolyte poly(allylamine) hydrochloride (PAH) for better bioconjugation. In the available sensor literature, the evaluation of the sensing layer stability after multiple sensing trials is almost nil. In this work, the sensor surface plasmonic performance after multiple sensing trials is evaluated. The results show that plasmonic biosensors with improved sensing performances can be developed by introducing carbon nanomaterials in PVA-silver nanocomposites. The developed sensor heads are used to sense Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) concentration changes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials are gaining importance in numerous material engineering applications and in turn develop promising and reliable solutions in medical, industrial and inhouse sectors [1–5]. Recent intense investigations on this material of the era, have contributed different synthesis methods and types of carbon nanostructures that find suitable applications in widely varying fields [6–10]. Of these, graphene and graphene oxide are two-dimensional carbon networks that find important contributions in biomedical fields and electronic device engineering [11–14]. Various novel synthesis methods gave way to the development of more confined and efficient carbon nanostructures like Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), Single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), and Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [15–20]. The major material engineering findings establish that these carbon nanostructures are strength-enhancing elements in polymer-based nanocomposite matrices [21–23].

Polymer-dispersed plasmonic metal nanoparticles are prominent in bio and chemical sensing [23–25]. The ability of the polymer nanocomposites to form a plasmonic film on the sensing substrate ensures both precise sensing performance as well as a durable sensor surface. Reliable and repeatable sensor performance is essential to reuse the surface for a wide range of applications. Initially, solution-based nanoparticles were dip-coated on the sensing surfaces, and multiple uses of such surfaces showed delamination of the sensing layer, leading to degradation in the sensor performance. At this point, a polymer-based nanocomposite matrix has emerged as a suitable sensing surface layer, and further investigations have led to optimizing them for reliable plasmonic sensing performance [26].

The reported results on carbon nanostructures interspersed in the polymer matrix have gained interest as the polymer film with improved stability is achieved [27]. Experimental investigations are hence carried out to check the effect of carbon nanostructures introduced into the polymer silver nanocomposite matrix. The objective of the present studies focuses on whether the polymer molecular chains acting as the reducing medium to generate the silver nanoparticles are also getting accommodated with traces of carbon nanostructures and provide the plasmonic sensing capability along with the improved strength to the coated sensor surface.

In the present experimental investigations, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is the polymer that acts as the reducing medium of silver nitrate (AgNO3) to generate silver nanoparticles of <20 nm size. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized by chemical method and dispersed in the PVA-silver nanocomposite. The CQD-strengthened silver nanocomposite is coated on the unclad U-bent fiber, as detailed in the procedure discussed in Section 3. Similarly, the PVA-silver nanoparticle medium is dispersed with multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), and the resulting solution is coated on the unclad U-bent optical fiber surface. Both the sensor surfaces are exposed to Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) concentration changes, and the sensing capabilities are separately monitored.

Serum albumins (SA) are the most abundant proteins in the blood plasma of vertebrates. Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) is extracted from the blood plasma of cows, and Human Serum Albumin (HSA) is the analog serum albumin found in humans. The serum albumins play a major role in controlling the transportation of vital elements like vitamins, enzymes, and hormones throughout the body and keep the osmotic pressure balanced. It is a very stable globular protein with 585 amino acids and a molecular mass of 66 kDa. The normal albumin level in blood plasma is in the range 3.4 to 5.4 g/dL. Sensing the quantity of serum albumin is important as variations in its measure from the normal range can cause significant health issues. Practical, highly sensitive, and stable sensing devices are hence required for estimating the concentration of serum albumins. The sensor surfaces developed here measure BSA concentration and evaluate the sensing performances.

Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA, A2153 lyophilized powder ≥96 %), Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS, P5368 pH 7.4), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) 99 % hydrolyzed, Silver nitrate (AgNO3), Ethylenediamine (≥99 %), Citric acid (anhydrous), (Poly (allylamine) Hydrochloride (PAH) (all chemicals purchased from Sigma), Plastic Clad Silica fiber (from Polymicro Technologies).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) in serum albumin sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The PVA film-coated surface is found to be more resistant to environmental impacts and protects the substrate when traces of carbon nanostructures are introduced. The capability of the polymer matrix to host the AgNO3 reduction process and generate fine silver nanoparticles having a size of <20 nm makes it a favorable choice for plasmonic sensing surface fabrication. Investigations are going on to realize the multiple real-world applications of carbon quantum dot dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposites. The experimental studies undertaken here try to evaluate the bio-sensing capability of carbon quantum dot dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite and its advantages.

5 wt% of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution is prepared in 100 ml of distilled water (for the complete dissolution of PVA, three to four hours of magnetic stirring is required @ 80 °C). 0.03 g of AgNO3 is dissolved in 5 ml of distilled water. The AgNO3 solution is added dropwise to the dissolved PVA solution that is kept under stirring at room temperature. After this, the temperature of the resulting solution is increased to 100 °C by placing it in an oil bath and stirred vigorously for 1 h, keeping it in the dark. The transparent solution slowly turns to yellow colour, indicating the formation of silver nanoparticles. The absorption spectrum of the PVA-silver nanocomposite is given in Fig. 1 . Jasco V-770 is used for taking the absorption spectrum.

One-pot synthesis of carbon quantum dots is performed using a microwave-assisted method. 1 ml ethylenediamine is added to 1 gm of citric acid. This combination is kept in microwave-safe glassware with proper sealing and placed inside the microwave oven. The oven is operated at 800 Watts for 3 min. After this procedure, readily usable CQDs are obtained in the glass vessel. The absorption (Jasco V-770 Absorption Spectrometer) and PL emission spectra (Varian-Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer) of the synthesized CQDs are given in Figs. 2 & 3
. The blue emission from the synthesized CQDs under UV illumination is shown in the inset of Fig. 3.

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the synthesized carbon dots confirms the presence of CQDs with peaks corresponding to the vibrational modes of of the OH and N-H (3434 cm−1), C-N (2938 cm−1), C-O-C (1643 cm−1), CH2 (1395 cm−1) respectively (Fig. 4 ).
The peaks in the FTIR spectrum indicate the presence of carboxyl, hydroxyl as well as amino groups associated with the CQDs.
The synthesized and characterized CQDs are used to fabricate the sensor heads, as detailed in the procedure given below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CQD functionalized sensor heads
ce:section.ce:para: Fiber optic U-bent sensor heads are fabricated for the sensing purpose. Here, 400 µm plastic clad silica fiber is bent into a U shape as mentioned in the procedure given in reference [28]. The unclad U-bent fiber is cleaned by dipping it in piranha solution (H2SO4:H2O2 3:1) for 10 min. The U-bent surface is now washed with distilled water several times. The cleaned U-bent surface is dried using a flow of nitrogen gas. The U-bent fiber surface is now ready for PVA nanocomposite coating.

0.002 wt% of CQD is dissolved in 5 ml milli-Q water. 1 ml of the dissolved CQD is added to the PVA-silver nanocomposite prepared, and the solution is stirred well @ 700 rpm for 4 h. The absorption spectrum of the resulting solution is given in Fig. 5 .
The spectrum shows peaks corresponding to the dispersed CQD modified PVA matrix (300 nm & 223 nm) and the plasmonic absorption peak (422 nm) corresponding to the dispersed silver nanoparticles.

The viscous solution of the CQD dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite is used for the cleaned fiber optic sensor surface coating. The U bent fiber is dipped in the solution for 24 h. Then, the fiber is carefully taken out and dried in hot air oven for 10 min @ 90 °C. This process is repeated three times. The CQD functionalized sensor heads are further used for the BSA sensing studies.

The sensing measurements show that the adhesion of the BSA molecules upon the sensor surface is ineffective as both the sensor surface and the sensing samples possess like charges. Hence, the sensor surface is further integrated with a cationic polyelectrolyte (poly allylamine hydrochloride (PAH)). For this purpose, 2 mg/ml PAH is dissolved in distilled water. The fabricated sensor heads are dipped in this solution for 20 min. Then the sensor heads are allowed to dry in air. The PAH integrated sensor heads are now used for the serum albumin sensing measurements.
In addition, separate sensor heads are also fabricated by dispersing CQD in PVA and PVA-silver nanocomposite, for comparing the fiber absorbance in each case.

The simple experimental setup used for the sensing measurements is shown in Fig. 6 . The broadband source (Yogokawa-AQ4305) with an emission spectrum in the wavelength range (400–1800) nm is used as the input source. The sensor output is monitored for selective plasmonic absorption using a fiber optic spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics (HR 4000)) with a wavelength resolution of 0.28 nm. This device is integrated with a PC that runs Spectrasuite software for displaying the real time spectral changes.
BSA samples purchased from Sigma are used as such for the sensing measurements without further purification. The purchased sample is stored in a refrigerator, maintained at a temperature 2–8 °C.

100 µM BSA stock solution (20 ml) is prepared in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) of pH 7.4. The prepared stock solution is kept below 8 °C. The desired concentrations of the solution ranging between (0–1000) nM are obtained by dilution. The sensor heads are dipped in the diluted solutions and the corresponding absorption changes are recorded for each concentration.

Fig. 7 shows the fiber absorbance obtained after coating the fiber surface with CQD dispersed PVA, PVA-silver nanocomposite, and the CQD functionalized PVA-silver nanocomposite separately. The absorbance spectrum is recorded by the fiber optic spectrophotometer. Fig. 7 shows a clear plasmonic absorbance peak in the CQD-dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite-coated fiber at 494 nm. There is a redshift observed compared to the PVA-silver nanocomposite-coated fiber absorbance peak at 482 nm. The redshift associated with the plasmonic peak is attributed to the increase in the dielectric constant of the coated film by incorporating the CQD along with the silver-dispersed PVA matrix.

The CQD functionalized PVA-silver nanocomposite-coated fiber sensor surfaces are now subjected to BSA sensing measurements. The sensor absorbance response with respect to BSA concentration changes is recorded by the fiber optic spectrophotometer. Fig. 8 shows the absorbance spectra of CQD functionalized PVA-silver nanocomposite sensor heads for BSA concentration changes.

The sensor surface response to different BSA concentrations shows a set of smaller peaks in the wavelength range (400–500) nm corresponding to the plasmonic response and an unshifted broader and higher peak at 657 nm corresponding to the effective surface absorbance due to the presence of CQD dispersed in PVA matrix in BSA solutions.

This result shows that the sensor surface does not exhibit a systematic plasmonic peak shift with respect to the concentration changes. This indicates the ineffective attachment of the BSA molecules to the sensor surface due to the negative surface charge possessed by both the sample and the sensor surface. An intermediate polycationic layer can improve the adhesion of the BSA samples to the sensor surface, and for this purpose, PAH integrated sensor surface is fabricated.

The sensor response shows that a concentration-dependent absorbance peak shift is observed for the BSA concentration changes from 500 nM to 1000 nM. A maximum of 30 nm peak shift is measured for the 500 nM concentration changes. Incorporating the PAH layer has significantly improved the sensing performance of the sensor head. Fig. 11 shows the concentration-dependent absorbance wavelength peak shift for the sensor response. The linear response of the wavelength shift with respect to the BSA concentration change indicates that the sensor surface is effective in measuring serum albumin concentration change in the required operational range with a measured sensitivity of 0.059 nm/nM.

The fast response of the sensor is another essential feature for real-time applications. The switching response of the sensor is recorded and is given in Fig. 12 . The result shows that on insertion of the sample, the sensor responds in 5 ms, and removing the sample from the proximity of the sensor surface leads to regain the initial operational feature in 57 ms. This shows a fast on–off response of the sensor.

Usually, when the sensor head is subjected to multiple trials of operations, performance degradation is observed due to the delamination of the plasmonic sensing layer. Fig. 13 shows the fiber absorbance of a sensor head coated with PAH-integrated PVA-silver nanocomposite. The absorbance is measured for this sensor head initially (i.e., before inserting it into any serum albumin medium) and after 20 trials.

However, in the case of the sensor-head functionalized with CQD, the plasmonic sensing response of the fiber remains almost the same with peak absorbance wavelength shift <±2 nm, after multiple sensing trials, as shown in Fig. 14 . In Fig. 15 , the reusability of the sensor-head is shown for different concentration sensing measurements. After rinsing the sensor head in each trial, <±1 nm wavelength shift is observed. These results indicate that the sensor remains reliable for multiple sensing trials by dispersing CQD in PVA-silver nanocomposite, in addition to enhancing the serum albumin sensing capability.

Multiple sensor probes are checked for the BSA concentration sensing for the reproducibility of the sensor performance. Fig. 16 shows the performance of three different sensor heads functionalized with CQD in PVA-silver polymer matrix.
For the three sensor-heads under trial for a particular BSA concentration (600 nM) the peak absorbance wavelength shows excellent repeatability and the wavelength shift measured is lesser than 1 nm.

Table 1 summarizes the plasmonic peak wavelength shift associated with the CQD dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite coated sensor head under different measurement trials. The results show plasmonic peak change of the sensor head on CQD dispersion, peak absorbance wavelength change with respect to BSA concentration, the reliable performance, reusability and reproducibility of the sensor head on CDQ dispersion in the metal-polymer silver nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) in serum albumin sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) are yet another promising carbon nanostructure finding applications in diverse fields of industry. Incorporating MWCNTs in polymers like polyvinyl alcohol as nanofillers has significant material engineering scope, mainly as strength-enhancing elements in the polymer matrix. The present investigations analyze the advantages of dispersing MWCNTs in PVA-silver nanocomposite film as a biosensor surface.

Purchased MWCNTs are used as the nanofillers in PVA-silver nanocomposite. XRD of the purchased MWCNT is taken and is shown in Fig. 17 . Initially, 0.002 wt% of MWCNT dissolved in distilled water is dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol. The solution is continuously stirred for 8 h. The absorption spectrum of the solution is taken using the Jasco V-770 absorption spectrometer and is given in Fig. 18 . The XRD pattern and the absorption spectrum confirm the characteristics of MWCNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MWCNT dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite sensor-head fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: The characterized MWCNT is used for dispersing in PVA-silver nanocomposite. The PVA-silver nanocomposite is prepared as given in section 3.1. 1 ml of the prepared 0.002 wt% of the MWCNT solution is added to the PVA-silver nanocomposite. The solution is stirred continuously for 8 h. The absorption spectrum of the MWCNT-dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite is given in Fig. 19 . The absorption peaks confirm the plasmonic absorbance and the presence of MWCNT nanofillers in the polymer matrix. This viscous solution is used for coating the prepared unclad cleaned U-bent fiber surface. The sensor head preparation is done as detailed in Section 3.3.

Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) image of the coated fiber surface is taken. Fig. 20 shows the image of the fiber surface coated with MWCNT-dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol. In Fig. 21 , MWCNT dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite coated fiber surface is shown. Here, due to the high surface reactivity of the MWCNTs the silver nanoparticles dispersed in PVA get decorated on the surface of the carbon nanotubes. The fiber surface is again coated with PAH, as detailed in Section 3.1, to ensure the adhesion between the sensor surface and the serum albumins.

The experimental setup used for the sensing measurements is the same as detailed in Section 3.4. The BSA sample preparation is also carried out as explained in Section 3.5. Fig. 22 shows the fiber absorbance spectra of the different fibers coated with MWCNT dispersed in PVA, PVA-silver nanocomposite, and MWCNT-dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite separately.

Fig. 23 shows the concentration sensing response of the sensor surface integrated with PAH and MWCNT dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite. The sensor can follow the concentration changes in BSA effectively. The peak wavelength shift with respect to BSA concentration changes is plotted and is given in Fig. 24 .

The sensitivity of the sensor in the linear response range of the plot is 0.1236 nm/nM. At higher concentrations (>800 nM), the corresponding shift is lesser, indicating saturating behaviour of the sensor.

The switching response of the sensor is measured and is given in Fig. 25 . A faster rise time of ∼5 ms is recorded on inserting the sensor head into the sample, and the retrieval time is recorded as 81 ms. This indicates that the sensor is responding faster to the serum albumin concentration changes and is suitable for real time sensing applications.

The MWCNT carbon nanofillers in PVA nanocomposite are a strength-enhancing element. The plasmonic sensing surface incorporated with MWCNT in PVA-silver nanocomposite is checked for reusability for multiple concentration sensing trials. Fig. 26 shows the performance of the PAH-integrated PVA-silver nanocomposite fiber surface absorbance after multiple trials. After 20 trials of BSA concentration sensing, the fiber response shows that the plasmonic layer delamination is taking place and the sensor performance is seriously affected.

Fig. 27 shows the plasmonic absorbance peak of the fiber integrated with PAH and PVA-silver nanocomposite dispersed with MWCNT. After multiple sensing trials, the initial and final plasmonic peaks are almost the same, indicating that the MWCNT nanofillers strengthen the PVA polymer film incorporated with the silver nanoparticles. The sensor surface retains the plasmonic sensing capability and durability by adding MWCNT nanostructures. In Fig. 28 , the sensor performance on reusing the sensor-head in concentration measurements is given. The sensor head shows excellent repeatability in multiple sensing trials with 0 nm peak shift.

Ensuring the reproducible behavior of the sensor head is also very important in establishing the sensor fabrication procedure for a particular application. Here, the BSA concentration sensing is repeated on different sensor heads fabricated under the same procedure. Fig. 29 shows the performance of three different MWCNT-dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite-coated sensor heads for a particular BSA concentration (600 nM). The sensor heads follow exactly the same response pattern indicating the reproducibility of the performance in BSA concentration sensing.

Table 2 shows the sensor performance based on the peak absorbance wavelength shift. Here, the absorbance wavelength shift associated with each measurement trial establishes the excellent repeatability, reusability and reproducibility of the sensor head on incorporating the MWCNT in the PVA-silver polymer matrix.

One-pot microwave-assisted synthesis method is adopted for CQD in these studies. The incorporation of CQDs in PVA-silver nanocomposites for electromagnetic shielding applications [29–31] reports the uniform dispersion of the CQDs in the PVA matrix and strong adhesion of the CQDs on silver nanoparticles dispersed in PVA-silver nanocomposite. In the present investigations, the physical blending method is adopted to disperse CQDs in the PVA-silver nanocomposite. Noncovalent interactions such as π-π interactions, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions are significant in the PVA-CQD composites prepared by the physical blending method [32]. PVA is a thermoplastic polymer, and by adding CQDs, crosslinking active sites are enabled in the polymer matrix due to the presence of active hydrogen and oxygen-rich functional groups in CQDs. Here, the FTIR spectrum given in Fig. 4 indicates that the synthesized CQD surfaces are enriched with the oxygen-containing functional groups. The presence of these carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups ensures the CQD interaction with metal ions and the continuous phase of the polymer matrix as well. The CQDs functionalized with carboxyl groups having a strong affinity to Ag+ ions are adsorbed onto the surface of the silver nanoparticles. The silver nanoparticles are hence prevented from getting agglomerated in the polymer matrix. The stability of the plasmonic absorption feature is thus ensured by the presence of the dispersed CQDs in the PVA film. Polymer films dispersed with CQDs are also reported to have enhanced film strength [33,34]. PVA-silver nanocomposite decorated with CQDs shows increased dielectric constant that, in turn, contributes to the higher refractive index sensing surface. The increased refractive index of the surface, in turn, effectively increases the extinction coefficient, which leads to increased plasmonic absorption [35]. The distribution of the CQDs on the PVA-dispersed silver nanoparticles offers a sensitive shift in the plasmonic absorption peaks with respect to the surrounding concentration changes. In serum albumin sensing, all these factors are positively contributing, but the sensor surface is becoming negatively charged altogether, and the like charge of the BSA sample keeps it away from the sensing surface proximity, and the sample becomes less adhesive to the sensor surface. This issue is sorted out by adding a bioconjugation element, poly allylamine hydrochloride (PAH) which is a polyelectrolyte cation, on the sensing surface. After the integration of the PAH layer on the CQD functionalized PVA-silver nanocomposite-coated sensor surface, the sensor started following the concentration changes of the BSA sample with better sensitivity. In addition to this, the CQD-dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite films become more hydrophobic, and this makes the sensor surface more resistant to water-absorbed swelling and degradation [36].

By dispersing MWCNT in PVA-silver nanocomposite matrix the optical, mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties are changed considerably. The delocalized bonds introduced by the available p electrons from the carbon atoms modify the metal-polymer film surface properties significantly. The FESEM image of the coated sensor surface shows the distribution of carbon nanotubes evenly on the fiber surface in the PVA matrix (Fig. 20). When the MWCNTs are dispersed in PVA-silver nanocomposite from the FESEM images of the coated fiber surface, it can be observed that silver nanoparticles are distributed on the carbon nanotube surfaces (Fig. 21). The attachment of silver nanoparticles on the surface of MWCNT makes the smooth tubular outer layer of carbon nanotubes rougher. This prevents the MWCNTs from getting agglomerated in the PVA matrix. As in the case of CQD dispersed PVA-nanocomposite, reported results show that MWCNT is also introducing a hydrophobic nature to the PVA film and enhances the water-resistant properties [37]. In addition, the MWCNT dispersion in PVA-silver nanocomposites improves the absorption capability due to the increased refractive index of the sensor surface [38–41]. The multiwall tubular structure provides increased surface area for the silver adhesion and, in turn, contributes to better sensing surface exposure to the biosamples [42]. As in the case of CQDs, PAH also plays a major role in bringing the BSA samples to the proximity of the sensor surface. The MWCNT's tubular structure gives inter-spatial nanoparticle distribution and diffused BSA adhesion [43]. This can be why the wavelength shift becomes less after the initial concentration measurements, and the sensor surface tends to saturate at higher concentrations.

BSA adhesion to the sensor surface is attributed to the presence of the positively charged polyelectrolyte PAH. The electrostatic interaction between PAH and BSA leads to the binding of the proteins to the sensor surface. For the plasmonic sensing of the protein proximity of the analyte to the sensor surface in nanometric depth scale is necessary. This is ensured by the binding of BSA by the polyelectrolyte. Reported results show that the adhesion between the BSA and PAH surface is at thermal equilibrium, and maximum binding occurs at a pH 7.4 [44].

The switching response of the CQD and MWCNT dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite coated sensor heads shows that the sensor heads function based on localized surface plasmon resonance. This ensures the real-time sensing capability of both sensor types. Table 3 summarizes the sensing parameters of the CQD and MWCNT-incorporated silver nanocomposite sensor heads.

In plasmonic sensors, the merits of carbon nanostructures in enhancing the strength and durability of the polymer films are achieved by incorporating the CQD and MWCNT in PVA-silver nanocomposite and using it as the sensing surface. The sensitivity of the MWCNT incorporated sensor is better compared to the CQD dispersed sensor head. However, the CQD dispersed PVA-silver nanocomposite sensor head provides better detection accuracy. For the MWCNT incorporated sensor head, the measured sensing parameters remain valid up to a concentration range of 800 nM. In the case of CQD dispersed sensor head for the entire operation range of (500–800) nM concentration change, the sensor response is linear. The plasmonic peak of the fiber sensor remains unchanged even after multiple sensing trials, and this ensures the reuse of the sensor surface for practical sensing applications.

Table 4 gives a comparison of the performance parameters of different types of fibre optic plasmonic sensors. The mentioned sensors are recent investigations basically conducted for bio-sample sensing applications. For example, in the case of the fiber optic waveflex biosensor, a flexible W-shaped optical fiber (WFOB) is developed for histamine detection [45]. Here, the multiple tapering on the core of the singlemode fiber forms a W-shaped sensing region, where the core modes are effectively coupled to the clad region. The core surface is attached with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the LSPR excitation and then for improved sensing modified with molybdenum disulfide nanoparticles (MoS2-NPs). The sensing surface is finally modified with the enzyme diamine oxidase (DAO) to selectively attach the histamine. The sensor parameters are evaluated for the performance characterization as given in Table 4. Similarly, some other recently developed SPR sensor parameters are also compared with the present studies [46–49].

Table 4 shows that the present work provides comparable performance parameters with respect to the other developed sensors and, in addition, evaluated for the sensing layer stability after multiple sensing trials. Here, it is established that the incorporation of CQD and MWCNT effectively improved the sensor layer stability, which is very important in the development of the sensor as an instrument.

This manuscript explains the experimental methods to achieve the positive features of the carbon nanostructures dispersed in polymer matrix incorporated with the sensing capability of silver nanocomposites in biosensing applications. Normally in the available literature of sensor experimental studies, sensor surface stability after multiple usage is not reported. Here the sensing surface performance is evaluated after multiple trials. The major issue associated with the plasmonic metal-polymer nanocomposite bio sensing surfaces are the performance degradation due to the delamination of the sensing layer when exposed to the bio sample solutions on multiple trials. In this work, the carbon nanomaterials such as Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are incorporated in the polymer-silver matrix. The carbon nanostructures incorporated in the polymer-silver nanocomposite enhances the biosensing layer durability by preventing the swelling and delamination of the plasmonic layer due to the polymer hydrophilicity. This is a new direction that ensures the sensor surface reusability and thus promising in the aspect of reliable biosensor instrument development. Incorporation of CQDs and MWCNTs in PVA-silver nanocomposites have also increased the protein sensing capabilities in addition to providing the sensor surface stability and reliability.

The new surfaces that have been developed are made effective in serum albumin sensing applications. This is achieved by integrating a polyelectrolyte cation layer (PAH) as the outer surface on the CQD/MWCNT functionalized polymer-silver biosensing surface. BSA sensing capability of the surface is evolved by this process and all the other plasmonic sensing features are maintained. The sensor surface has become more durable and reusable for multiple sensing trials by incorporating the carbon nanomaterials. A well-developed procedure in CQD functionalized sensor head development beginning from the synthesis of CQD is described in the manuscript. The final results in BSA sensing applications with the evaluation of sensor performance parameters like sensitivity, Figure of Merit (FoM), linearity and Limit of Detection (LoD) are given. Similarly, the MWCNT functionalized sensor surface fabrication, characterization and the better sensing capability of the sensor surface in BSA sensing are established. The analysis of the developed sensor surface performance with the other fibre optic plasmonic biosensors reveal that the developed sensor surfaces have comparable sensing parameters along with improved sensor surface durability. In a broader perspective, for sensor instrument development based on plasmonic bio sensing principle, better performing sensor surface can be developed by incorporating carbon nanostructures in polymer-metal nanocomposites.

V.C. Priyamvada: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sony Udayan: Methodology, Conceptualization. K.R. Vijesh: Validation, Formal analysis. V.P.N. Nampoori: Writing – review & editing, Validation. P. Radhakrishnan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
